name: Leopard
summary: Leopards are spotted wild cats that hunt alone across Africa and Asia. They climb trees, drag prey into branches, and are famous for their golden coats and...
metaTitle: Leopard Facts | Habitat, Diet, Spots & Photos
metaDescription: Discover leopard facts with photos, forest habitat, hunting diet, cub life, and conservation. Learn where leopards live, what they eat, and why they climb...
speed: Up to 36 mph in short bursts
funFacts: Every leopard has a unique spot pattern—like a fingerprint.
Leopards can drag prey heavier than themselves high into trees.
Black leopards still have spots—you can see them in bright light.
Leopards are strong swimmers and sometimes hunt near water.
A leopard's tail helps it balance when climbing and leaping.
Leopard cubs stay with their mother for about two years.

faq.question: What is a leopard?
faq.answer: A leopard is a spotted wild cat in the same genus as lions and tigers. Leopards live across Africa and parts of Asia and hunt alone.

faq.question: Where do leopards live?
faq.answer: Leopards live in forests, woodlands, savannas, and mountains from sub-Saharan Africa to India and Southeast Asia.

faq.question: What do leopards eat?
faq.answer: Leopards are carnivores that eat antelope, deer, wild pigs, monkeys, and other animals they can catch.

faq.question: Are leopards endangered?
faq.answer: Leopards are listed as Vulnerable. Habitat loss, fewer prey animals, and conflict with people threaten many populations.

faq.question: Do leopards live in groups?
faq.answer: Adult leopards usually live alone. Mothers raise cubs until the young cats can hunt on their own.

faq.question: Why do leopards climb trees?
faq.answer: Trees give leopards a safe place to rest, watch for prey, and store food away from lions and hyenas.

faq.question: How is a leopard different from a cheetah?
faq.answer: Leopards are heavier, stronger climbers, and have rosette spots instead of solid black dots. Cheetahs are built for speed on open ground.
